exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar and Vocabulary Work, Task 1: Conditional (if) sentences

promptParts.before: If I
promptParts.after: (stay) in Barlinek, I would have found a new girlfriend.

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'would have found' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had stayed', describing an unfulfilled past condition.

promptParts.before: He would do more to help the poor if he

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional (hypothetical present) uses simple past in the if-clause. With 'be', the traditional/formal form for all persons is 'were' (not 'was'): 'if he were the Pope'.

promptParts.before: If he goes to London on a business trip, he

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional (real future possibility): if-clause in simple present ('goes') pairs with 'will' + infinitive in the main clause: 'he will visit Soho'.

promptParts.before: We won't go to the film unless they

questions.grammarRule: 'Unless' means 'if...not' and follows the same pattern as First Conditional if-clauses: simple present, not 'will': 'unless they arrive'.

promptParts.before: She
promptParts.after: (buy) a new car if she had had the money.

questions.grammarRule: Since the if-clause is 'had had' (past perfect, Third Conditional), the main clause needs 'would have' + past participle: 'would have bought', describing an unfulfilled past possibility.

promptParts.before: If Yoko were me, she

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional main clause uses 'would' + infinitive, matching the hypothetical if-clause 'were': 'she would go to Manchester'.

promptParts.before: They will talk to Jacek if he

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause uses simple present, agreeing with the subject 'he': 'if he comes' (not 'will come' or 'came').

promptParts.before: Bożena will come to work 30 minutes late if her child

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause uses simple present with correct subject-verb agreement: 'her child misses' (third person singular -s).

promptParts.before: If Peter
promptParts.after: (think) twice, he wouldn't have made such a stupid mistake.

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'wouldn't have made' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had thought'.

promptParts.before: Kasia
promptParts.after: (become) a university lecturer if she studies hard.

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional main clause uses 'will' + infinitive, matching the if-clause 'studies' (simple present): 'Kasia will become...'.

promptParts.before: If they
promptParts.after: (know) all the facts, they would have found the defendant guilty.

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'would have found' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had known'.

promptParts.before: Unless you
promptParts.after: (hurry up), we will never arrive on time.

questions.grammarRule: 'Unless' (= if...not) follows the same pattern as First Conditional if-clauses: simple present, not 'will': 'unless you hurry up'.

promptParts.before: If I were in charge, I

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional main clause uses 'would' + infinitive, matching the hypothetical if-clause 'were': 'I would change...'.

promptParts.before: He will take his daughter out to dinner, if she

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause uses simple present with correct subject-verb agreement: 'if she comes' (not 'will come' or 'came').

promptParts.before: If I hadn't known better, I

questions.grammarRule: Since the if-clause is 'hadn't known' (past perfect negative, Third Conditional), the main clause needs 'would have' + past participle: 'would have trusted'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar and Vocabulary Work, Task 2: Tenses

promptParts.before: We
promptParts.after: (write) an English test at the moment.

questions.grammarRule: 'At the moment' signals an action happening right now — present continuous: 'are writing'.

promptParts.before: We never
promptParts.after: (write) more than six tests per year.

questions.grammarRule: A general habitual fact ('never... per year') uses present simple: 'we never write'.

promptParts.before: Two weeks from now we

questions.grammarRule: A planned future event with a specific time frame ('two weeks from now') uses 'going to': 'are going to write'.

promptParts.before: Last night I

questions.grammarRule: 'Last night' is a specific past time marker, so simple past is needed: 'went'.

promptParts.before: Last night I went to the cinema. I
promptParts.after: an interesting film, but my friend and I were so tired that we didn't have any fun.

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative of a completed sequence of past events: 'saw'.

promptParts.before: Last night I went to the cinema. I saw an interesting film, but my friend and I
promptParts.after: so tired that we didn't have any fun.

questions.grammarRule: 'My friend and I' together form a plural subject ('we'), so the past tense of 'be' is 'were', not 'was'.

promptParts.before: Last night I went to the cinema. I saw an interesting film, but my friend and I were so tired that we

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative: negative simple past is 'didn't have'.

promptParts.before: In the morning we usually
promptParts.after: cornflakes and drink tea. Last Sunday we had a brunch and ate muffins and strawberries.

questions.grammarRule: 'Usually' signals a habitual routine — present simple: 'we usually eat'.

promptParts.before: In the morning we usually eat cornflakes and
promptParts.after: tea. Last Sunday we had a brunch and ate muffins and strawberries.

questions.grammarRule: This continues the habitual present simple routine: 'and drink tea'.

promptParts.before: In the morning we usually eat cornflakes and drink tea. Last Sunday we
promptParts.after: a brunch and ate muffins and strawberries.

questions.grammarRule: 'Last Sunday' is a specific past time marker, switching from the habitual present to simple past: 'we had a brunch'.

promptParts.before: In the morning we usually eat cornflakes and drink tea. Last Sunday we had a brunch and

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative describing what happened last Sunday: 'and ate muffins'.

promptParts.before: Next Sunday I

questions.grammarRule: A stated future plan ('next Sunday') uses 'going to': 'am going to visit my grandma'.

promptParts.before: My family
promptParts.after: in Rodgau for a very long time.

questions.grammarRule: 'For a very long time' with no clear end signals an unfinished duration up to now — present perfect: 'has lived'.

promptParts.before: My family has lived in Rodgau for a very long time. My older brother

questions.grammarRule: 'Last Christmas' is a specific, finished past time marker — simple past is needed: 'moved', not present perfect.

promptParts.before: My older brother moved to Hamburg last Christmas. He

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative about a specific past sequence of events: 'found'.

promptParts.before: Before we
promptParts.after: to school we had eaten breakfast together.

questions.grammarRule: Of the two past actions, walking to school happened after eating, so it takes simple past: 'walked'.

promptParts.before: Before we walked to school we

questions.grammarRule: Eating breakfast happened before walking to school — the earlier of two past actions takes past perfect: 'had eaten'.

promptParts.before: After we
promptParts.after: our homework my friend and I went shopping.

questions.grammarRule: Finishing homework happened before going shopping — the earlier of two past actions takes past perfect: 'had finished'.

promptParts.before: After we had finished our homework my friend and I

questions.grammarRule: Going shopping is the later of the two past actions, so it takes simple past: 'went'.

promptParts.before: Yesterday we
promptParts.after: along Marktstraße when we met two more friends.

questions.grammarRule: 'When' introduces a shorter action (meeting friends) that interrupts a longer background action — the background action takes past continuous: 'were walking'.

promptParts.before: Yesterday we were walking along Marktstraße when we

questions.grammarRule: The shorter action that interrupts an ongoing background action takes simple past: 'met'.

promptParts.before: Yesterday we were walking along Marktstraße when we met two more friends. So all of us
promptParts.after: down at the ice cream parlour and enjoyed fantastic ice cream delights.

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative of sequential completed actions: 'sat down'.

promptParts.before: So all of us sat down at the ice cream parlour and

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative: 'and enjoyed fantastic ice cream delights'.

promptParts.before: We

questions.grammarRule: 'Just' + present perfect describes something completed a very short time ago, relevant to now: 'have just made'.

promptParts.before: My friends

questions.grammarRule: 'Yet' in a negative sentence about an expected-but-not-done action signals present perfect: 'haven't seen... yet'.

promptParts.after: the big red parrot last week?

questions.grammarRule: 'Last week' is a specific, finished past time marker, so the simple past question form is needed: 'Did you see...?', not present perfect.
'Ever' asking about life experience up to now signals present perfect: 'Have you ever eaten...?'.

promptParts.before: The last Olympic Games

questions.grammarRule: A specific past date ('in 2000') needs simple past: 'took place'.

promptParts.before: The last Olympic Games took place in Sydney in 2000. The games

questions.grammarRule: 'The games' is plural, and this continues the specific past narrative: 'were very successful'.

promptParts.before: Since then a lot more tourists

questions.grammarRule: 'Since then' points to a period that continues up to now — present perfect: 'have visited'.

promptParts.before: It normally

questions.grammarRule: 'Normally' signals a general habitual fact — present simple: 'it normally takes'.

promptParts.before: That's why lot of tourists
promptParts.after: to Australia. 22 hours in the air can be quite long.

questions.grammarRule: This continues the general present-tense fact: 'tourists don't fly'.

promptParts.before: A few weeks ago I
promptParts.after: my friends what they had seen in Australia.

questions.grammarRule: 'A few weeks ago' is a specific past time marker for the main reported action: simple past 'asked'.

promptParts.before: A few weeks ago I asked my friends what they

questions.grammarRule: Inside reported speech, an action that happened before the main past action (asking) takes past perfect: 'had seen'.

promptParts.before: They
promptParts.after: : 'We saw many koalas and kangaroos when we were travelling around Down Under.'

questions.grammarRule: Simple past reports what someone said at a specific past moment: 'they said'.

promptParts.before: They said: 'We
promptParts.after: many koalas and kangaroos when we were travelling around Down Under.'

questions.grammarRule: Inside the direct quote, this is straightforward simple past narrative: 'we saw'.

promptParts.before: 'We saw many koalas and kangaroos when we

questions.grammarRule: An ongoing background action ('when...') takes past continuous: 'were travelling'.

promptParts.before: Microsoft

questions.grammarRule: A current general fact uses present simple: 'Microsoft is a famous company'.

promptParts.before: Bill Gates
promptParts.after: millions of dollars with it so far.

questions.grammarRule: 'So far' signals present perfect, describing a total/result continuing up to now: 'has made'.

promptParts.before: All of you

questions.grammarRule: 'For five years' describing an ongoing activity that continues now uses present perfect continuous: 'have been learning'.

promptParts.before: I
promptParts.after: this grammar exercise will be easy for you.

questions.grammarRule: 'Hope' expressing a present opinion/wish uses simple present: 'I hope'.

promptParts.before: I hope this grammar exercise
promptParts.after: easy for you. Good luck with it!

questions.grammarRule: An uncertain future outcome takes 'will' + infinitive: 'will be easy for you'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar and Vocabulary Work, Task 3: Phrasal verbs (match each sentence with a noun)

promptParts.before: "This takes off and lands." This describes

questions.grammarRule: 'Take off' (of a plane) means to leave the ground and begin flying; 'land' means to arrive back on the ground. Only an aeroplane does both: this describes an aeroplane.

promptParts.before: "Words can be looked up in this." This describes

questions.grammarRule: 'Look up' means to search for information (like a word's meaning) in a reference source — a dictionary is exactly that.

promptParts.before: "You bring this up at a meeting." This describes

questions.grammarRule: 'Bring up' means to introduce a topic for discussion — you bring up a discussion point, not a physical object.

promptParts.before: "You should rest to get over this." This describes

questions.grammarRule: 'Get over' means to recover from something, typically used for an illness or a difficult experience: 'get over an illness'.

promptParts.before: "You take this off if it gets hot." This describes

questions.grammarRule: 'Take off' (clothing) means to remove it. You take off a jumper (sweater) when you're too warm.

promptParts.before: "It's not easy to bring them up well." This describes

questions.grammarRule: 'Bring up' (a person) means to raise/care for them from childhood — this refers to children, not objects.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar and Vocabulary Work, Task 4: Complete the sentences with the correct form of take or put

promptParts.before: I hope she didn't
promptParts.after: offence when I said the food was a bit salty.

questions.grammarRule: 'Take offence' is a fixed idiom meaning 'to feel insulted'. After 'didn't', the base form is needed: 'take', not 'took'.

promptParts.before: Don't
promptParts.after: any notice of Bob - he's only teasing you.

questions.grammarRule: 'Take notice of' is a fixed idiom meaning 'to pay attention to'. After 'don't', the base form is needed: 'take'.

promptParts.before: My mother's
promptParts.after: pressure on me to get married.

questions.grammarRule: 'Put pressure on someone' is a fixed idiom. After the contracted 'is' ('mother's putting'), the verb needs the -ing form: 'putting'.

promptParts.before: You have to

questions.grammarRule: 'Take risks' is a fixed idiom meaning 'to do something that might not succeed'. After 'have to', the base form is needed: 'take'.

promptParts.after: yourself in Mike's shoes - do you think you could do better?

questions.grammarRule: 'Put yourself in someone's shoes' is a fixed idiom meaning 'imagine being in their situation'. As a command (imperative), it uses the base form: 'Put yourself...'.

promptParts.before: I wish you would

questions.grammarRule: 'Put something first' is a fixed idiom meaning 'to prioritize it'. After the modal 'would', the base form is needed: 'put'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar and Vocabulary Work, Task 5: Match the understatements and exaggerations

promptParts.before: "She's a bit thirsty." The exaggerated version is:

options: She was totally out of order.
She can't stand the sight of him.
questions.grammarRule: 'Dying for' is a common exaggeration idiom meaning 'wanting something very much' — an exaggerated way of saying 'a bit thirsty'.

promptParts.before: "She's pretty well off." The exaggerated version is:

options: She was totally out of order.
She can't stand the sight of him.
questions.grammarRule: 'Loaded' is slang for 'very rich' — an exaggerated way of saying someone is 'well off' (comfortably wealthy).

promptParts.before: "She was a bit rude." The exaggerated version is:

options: She was totally out of order.
She can't stand the sight of him.
correctAnswers: She was totally out of order.
questions.grammarRule: 'Out of order' (describing behavior) means acting inappropriately/unacceptably — an exaggerated way of saying someone was 'a bit rude'.

promptParts.before: "She isn't keen on him." The exaggerated version is:

options: She can't stand the sight of him.
She was totally out of order.
correctAnswers: She can't stand the sight of him.
questions.grammarRule: 'Can't stand the sight of someone' means to strongly dislike them — an exaggerated way of saying someone 'isn't keen on' (doesn't like) another person.

promptParts.before: "She was a bit upset." The exaggerated version is:

options: She was totally out of order.
She can't stand the sight of him.
questions.grammarRule: 'Hit the roof' is an idiom meaning to become very angry — an exaggerated way of describing someone who was 'a bit upset'.

promptParts.before: "She's a bit chilly." The exaggerated version is:

options: She was totally out of order.
She can't stand the sight of him.
questions.grammarRule: 'Freezing' is a common exaggeration for feeling very cold — an exaggerated way of saying 'a bit chilly'.
